critique.summary: The article describes a game called Hostile Volume, where players must set and maintain the volume at exactly 25% to progress through various levels. The game features intentionally frustrating user interface elements and humorous feedback on player actions.
mainPoints: Players must set the volume to exactly 25% and hold it for 3 seconds to progress.
The game includes various levels with creative and frustrating mechanics.
critique.truthfulness: The claims made in the article about the game's mechanics and objectives appear to be accurate based on the provided content. The description of the gameplay aligns with the concept of intentionally bad UI design, which is a common theme in modern indie games.
considerations: The article does not mention the potential psychological effects of intentionally frustrating game mechanics on players.
There is no discussion about the game's reception or reviews from players.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around various experiences and opinions regarding bad volume control UIs, with users sharing anecdotes and insights about their interactions with such designs. Some comments highlight specific examples and offer critiques, while others share personal experiences with the game mentioned in the article.

comments.author: graypegg
comments.text: This is not an issue at all, but when ever I come across something like it, I like to poke at the frontend in dev tools...
comments.analysis: The comment provides a constructive perspective on the game's mechanics and offers a practical tip, showcasing a thoughtful engagement with the topic.

comments.author: jonathanlydall
comments.text: My favourite bad volume control was in Real Player around 1997 where changing the volume in the application actually changed...
comments.analysis: This comment shares a relevant anecdote about a historical example of poor UI design, contributing to the discussion effectively.

comments.author: Retr0id
comments.text: There are two types of volume slider I've encountered thus far, "too logarithmic", and "not logarithmic enough".
comments.analysis: The comment offers a succinct classification of volume slider types, which is insightful but lacks personal experience or examples.

comments.author: TheLNL
comments.text: Finished the game. It was fun to play. I got stuck for a while on the opposite level where the display doesn't update...
comments.analysis: This comment provides feedback on the game experience, though it is less analytical compared to others.

comments.author: jimkleiber
comments.text:
Got an error on Level 17, just a heads up.

Love the game, btw.
comments.analysis: While the comment alerts others to a potential issue, it lacks depth in analysis or broader context.

comments.author: jupin
comments.text: Laughed out loud but gave up at level 5
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a personal reaction but does not contribute much to the discussion's depth.

comments.author: danjl
comments.text: ...and, of course, there's really no need for a volume control in any app, since there's already a system volume...
comments.analysis: The comment raises a valid point about redundancy in volume controls, but it lacks specific examples or engagement with the game.

comments.author: DrSiemer
comments.text: Plenty of annoyance in here for sure. Looks like 17 cannot be finished on mobile though...
comments.analysis: This comment points out a specific issue with the game on mobile, but it could benefit from more elaboration.

comments.author: Findecanor
comments.text:
When clicking on this topic, I had anticipated it to be about GTK 3.x's messed up sliders.

This was more fun...
comments.analysis: While the comment reflects an expectation that adds context, it diverges from the main topic and lacks direct engagement.

comments.author: tobr
comments.text: Meanwhile, iPhone is still using this design https://xkcd.com/1884/
comments.analysis: This comment seems to be more of a joke referencing a comic rather than a serious contribution to the discussion.
